past_medical_history: Generalized anxiety disorder, diagnosed 8 years ago
Mild hypertension, diagnosed 3 years ago, well controlled
Remote episode of viral labyrinthitis 4 years ago, fully resolved

medications.frequency: as needed, up to three times daily

family_history: Mother diagnosed with dementia at age 78, type unspecified, lived in assisted living facility for last 5 years of life
Father died of myocardial infarction at age 70
Maternal aunt with progressive memory loss starting in her late 60s
One sister, age 55, recently evaluated for psychiatric symptoms including irritability and anxiety

social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.occupation: Retired high school art teacher, taught for 30 years, retired 2 years ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in single-story home
social_history.exercise: Daily 30-minute walks until 6 months ago when she began getting lost in familiar neighborhoods

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and year, but unsure of exact date (states month incorrectly). Cooperative and pleasant. Affect is mildly anxious. Speech is fluent with normal rate, rhythm, and prosody. No paraphasic errors. MMSE 24/30: lost 1 point on orientation to time, 0/3 on delayed recall, 1 point on serial sevens, 1 point on copying intersecting pentagons. Clock drawing test scored 2/5: numbers placed outside the circle, hands not pointing to correct time. Notably, the patient was unable to describe the spatial arrangement of objects on the desk and had difficulty identifying overlapping figures (Poppelreuter test).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, 3 mm, reactive to light and accommodation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual acuity 20/25 bilaterally (corrected). Confrontation visual fields full to finger counting in all quadrants bilaterally, but simultanagnosia elicited on bilateral simultaneous stimulation with visual extinction on the left. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick. Facial movements symmetric. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius full strength. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups of bilateral upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. No adventitious movements or myoclonus.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes. Impaired graphesthesia in bilateral hands (unable to identify numbers drawn on palms). Astereognosis in right hand (unable to identify key or coin by touch alone).
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No frontal release signs.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin testing intact. Rapid alternating movements normal. However, patient demonstrates significant difficulty with visually guided reaching — overshooting when asked to touch examiner's finger with eyes open (optic ataxia), and difficulty with smooth visual pursuit when tracking a moving object across midline.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal stride length and arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. However, patient hesitates at doorways and nearly collides with furniture when navigating an unfamiliar examination room.
neurological_exam.additional: Balint syndrome features present: simultanagnosia (cannot perceive more than one object at a time in a complex visual scene), optic ataxia (misreaching under visual guidance), and oculomotor apraxia (difficulty voluntarily directing gaze to peripheral targets). Gerstmann syndrome features partially present: finger agnosia (unable to identify individual fingers when touched with eyes closed), difficulty with left-right discrimination. Alexia present: patient cannot read more than individual letters despite preserved visual acuity. Ideomotor apraxia elicited when asked to pantomime brushing teeth and using scissors. No features of parkinsonism, myoclonus, or rapid cognitive fluctuation.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive difficulty reading, recognizing faces, and navigating familiar places over the past 8 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Lin Wei is a 62-year-old right-handed retired art teacher referred to the neurology outpatient clinic by her ophthalmologist for evaluation of progressive visual and cognitive complaints over approximately eight months. The patient initially presented to her optometrist six months ago complaining of increasing difficulty reading despite adequate corrected visual acuity. She described being unable to follow lines of text, frequently losing her place, and finding that words appeared to 'jump around' on the page. Her optometrist updated her prescription without significant improvement and referred her to an ophthalmologist, who found no retinal or optic nerve pathology and documented normal visual acuity and full visual fields on confrontation testing. Around the same time, her husband noticed that she was having increasing difficulty parking the car, frequently misjudging distances and bumping into curbs. She subsequently stopped driving after a minor fender bender in a parking lot three months ago. Over the past four months, the patient has developed progressive difficulty recognizing the faces of casual acquaintances, though she continues to recognize close family members by voice and context. She has become lost on three occasions while walking in her own neighborhood of twenty years, once requiring a neighbor to guide her home. She reports increasing difficulty dressing herself, specifically with buttoning shirts and orienting garments correctly, and has begun wearing pullover tops exclusively. Her husband reports that she recently could not find items on a cluttered table even when they were directly in front of her, describing that she seems to see individual objects but cannot take in the whole visual scene. Notably, the patient's conversational memory appears relatively preserved: she can recount recent family events, remembers appointments with prompting, and follows conversations without difficulty. Her husband reports that she does not repeat questions or forget recent conversations, which he finds puzzling given the severity of her other symptoms. There has been no sudden onset of any symptoms, no headache, no seizure activity, and no psychiatric symptoms beyond her longstanding anxiety, which her husband feels may have worsened modestly over the past several months. She has experienced no hallucinations, no changes in personality, and no motor symptoms. Her sleep pattern has been normal without REM sleep behavior disorder. There have been no recent infections, no weight loss, and no systemic symptoms. Her anxiety has increased somewhat in the context of these difficulties, but her mood has remained stable. The family history is notable for dementia in her mother and a maternal aunt, and her younger sister was recently evaluated for new-onset irritability and anxiety.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz, symmetric, well-organized alpha rhythm with normal anterior-posterior gradient
background.reactivity: Normal reactivity to eye opening and closing
background.state_changes: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves and sleep spindles

findings.location: Bilateral posterior regions, left greater than right
findings.clinical_correlation: Could represent early posterior cortical dysfunction or normal drowsiness variant. Not epileptiform.

artifacts.comment: Intermittent, does not obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: No significant change in background activity
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 8-14 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Largely normal EEG for age. Intermittent mild excess posterior theta activity is a nonspecific finding that could reflect early posterior cortical dysfunction or normal drowsiness. No periodic discharges, triphasic waves, or epileptiform activity identified. No features suggestive of prion disease or status epilepticus.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording. Longer or overnight monitoring could provide additional information.
recommended_actions: Consider repeat EEG with extended recording if clinical concern for seizures or prion disease persists
Neuropsychological testing recommended for further cognitive characterization

findings.location: Bilateral parieto-occipital cortex, posterior cingulate, and precuneus

signal_characteristics.T1: Volume loss with widened cortical sulci in the parieto-occipital regions bilaterally, left slightly greater than right
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No abnormal signal hyperintensity in the affected cortical regions. No restricted diffusion.
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion. No cortical ribboning pattern.

findings.location: Periventricular and deep white matter, bilateral frontal and parietal lobes

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Confluent periventricular caps and rims with early confluent deep white matter lesions
signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense corresponding lesions in periventricular regions

signal_characteristics.coronal_T1: Minimal widening of the choroid fissure bilaterally. Hippocampal volume within normal limits for age.

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Low-normal for age, bilateral (22nd percentile left, 28th percentile right)
volumetrics.parieto_occipital_cortical_thickness: Reduced bilaterally (5th percentile), left greater than right

additional_observations: Mild generalized cortical atrophy, age-appropriate, with disproportionate posterior predominance
No mass lesions or abnormal enhancement following gadolinium administration
Major intracranial vessels demonstrate normal flow voids
Small vessel disease changes as described above, moderate for stated age
mri.impression: Disproportionate bilateral parieto-occipital and posterior cingulate cortical atrophy with relative preservation of medial temporal structures. Moderate periventricular and deep white matter disease (Fazekas grade 2), which could suggest a vascular contribution to the clinical picture. No restricted diffusion to suggest prion disease. No enhancement or mass lesion. The posterior-predominant atrophy pattern, in the appropriate clinical context, raises concern for a neurodegenerative process including posterior cortical atrophy, though vascular cognitive impairment and other etiologies should be considered.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy (PCA), typically an atypical Alzheimer's variant
Vascular cognitive impairment with posterior predominance
Lewy body dementia with visual predominant presentation

recommended_actions: Consider amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers for Alzheimer's pathology confirmation
Consider FDG-PET to assess metabolic pattern
Follow-up MRI in 6-12 months to assess progression

findings: No left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria
ecg.interpretation: Normal ECG. No arrhythmia or conduction abnormality identified.
ecg.clinical_correlation: No cardiac etiology suggested for the patient's presenting symptoms.

Dementia_Screen.clinical_significance: Low-normal. Consider methylmalonic acid level if clinical suspicion persists.
Rules out syphilis as a cause of cognitive decline.

labs.interpretation: All laboratory values within normal limits. CBC, BMP, thyroid function, vitamin B12, folate, RPR, and HIV screening are unremarkable. No metabolic, infectious, or inflammatory etiology identified for the patient's cognitive and visual symptoms. The low-normal vitamin B12 level is unlikely to account for the clinical presentation but methylmalonic acid could be checked for completeness.

Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Mild impairment; points lost on orientation, recall, visuospatial copying
Moderate impairment. Significant deficits in visuospatial/executive (1/5), attention (4/6), and delayed recall (2/5).
Severely impaired visuoconstructional ability. Fragmented reproduction with loss of configural organization. Consistent with posterior cortical dysfunction.
Severely impaired, though largely attributable to poor initial encoding secondary to visuoconstructional deficit rather than a primary memory storage deficit.
Significantly slow. Reflects impaired visuospatial scanning and visual attention rather than primary motor slowing.
Severely impaired. Difficulty likely compounded by visuospatial deficits in addition to set-shifting demands.
Mildly impaired. Errors predominantly on visually complex items, suggesting visual perceptual contribution to naming difficulty rather than primary semantic deficit.
Low-normal to mildly impaired. Encoding is mildly reduced, but pattern shows benefit from cueing and recognition, suggesting retrieval difficulty rather than storage failure.
Low-normal. Mild retrieval weakness but recognition memory intact (15/16), suggesting relatively preserved hippocampal memory consolidation.
Severely impaired. Consistent with bilateral parieto-occipital dysfunction.
Severely impaired. Inability to mentally integrate fragmented visual objects is consistent with simultanagnosia.
Normal phonemic fluency, indicating preserved frontal-executive language networks.
Normal semantic fluency, further supporting preserved temporal lobe language function.

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile demonstrates a striking dissociation between severely impaired visuospatial and visuoperceptual abilities and relatively preserved verbal memory, language fluency, and auditory attention. The pattern is highly characteristic of posterior cortical atrophy (PCA). Key features include: (1) severe visuoconstructional impairment, (2) impaired spatial perception and judgment of line orientation, (3) impaired visual organization consistent with simultanagnosia, (4) relatively preserved episodic verbal memory with intact recognition, (5) preserved phonemic and semantic fluency. This profile localizes dysfunction to bilateral parieto-occipital association cortex with relative sparing of medial temporal and frontal regions. The pattern is atypical for classic amnestic Alzheimer's disease, vascular dementia, or frontotemporal dementia. It is most consistent with PCA, which is most commonly an atypical phenotypic variant of Alzheimer's disease pathology.
abnormal_values_summary: Severely impaired: Rey-Osterrieth Copy (8/36), Judgment of Line Orientation (12/30), Hooper VOT (11/30), Trail Making A (95s) and B (210s)
Moderately impaired: MoCA (19/30), MMSE (24/30), Boston Naming Test (52/60)
Mildly impaired: CVLT-II learning and recall (borderline, with intact recognition)
Preserved: Letter fluency, category fluency, digit span, auditory working memory

signal_characteristics.cortical_retention: Elevated cortical-to-cerebellar standardized uptake value ratio (SUVr) of 1.62 (positive threshold: >1.11)
signal_characteristics.distribution: Diffuse cortical amyloid deposition involving bilateral frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital cortices. Highest uptake in posterior cingulate, precuneus, and lateral parietal cortex.
signal_characteristics.regional_pattern: Posterior-predominant amyloid burden consistent with clinical presentation

additional_observations: Loss of gray-white matter distinction in bilateral parieto-occipital regions on the low-dose CT component, consistent with cortical atrophy
Cerebellar reference region demonstrates expected low uptake
No focal structural abnormality on CT component
output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. Diffuse cortical amyloid-beta deposition is present, with a pattern consistent with Alzheimer's disease neuropathology. The elevated cortical SUVr of 1.62 is clearly above the positivity threshold. In the context of posterior cortical atrophy syndrome, this finding supports a diagnosis of posterior cortical atrophy due to Alzheimer's disease.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Incidental amyloid positivity in the setting of a non-AD posterior cortical syndrome (e.g., corticobasal degeneration with co-pathology)

recommended_actions: Correlate with CSF biomarkers if additional confirmation is desired
Consider tau PET to characterize the distribution of tau pathology
Initiate discussion regarding potential disease-modifying therapies given confirmed AD pathology

signal_characteristics.hypometabolism: Marked bilateral temporoparietal and occipital hypometabolism, left greater than right. Precuneus and posterior cingulate also show significantly reduced FDG uptake.
signal_characteristics.preserved_regions: Frontal cortex demonstrates relatively preserved metabolic activity. Primary motor and sensory cortex are preserved. Subcortical structures including basal ganglia and thalami show normal metabolism.
signal_characteristics.medial_temporal: Hippocampal and medial temporal metabolism relatively preserved bilaterally, which is atypical for classic amnestic AD.

additional_observations: The metabolic pattern shows posterior-predominant cortical hypometabolism with frontal and medial temporal sparing
This pattern is characteristic of posterior cortical atrophy and differs from the typical temporoparietal pattern of classic amnestic Alzheimer's disease
No focal hypermetabolic lesion to suggest neoplastic process
output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates marked bilateral parieto-occipital and posterior cingulate hypometabolism with relative preservation of frontal, medial temporal, and subcortical metabolic activity. This pattern is characteristic of posterior cortical atrophy and strongly supports a neurodegenerative etiology affecting posterior cortical networks. The sparing of medial temporal metabolism is consistent with the clinical observation of relatively preserved episodic memory.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy due to Alzheimer's disease
Lewy body dementia with posterior predominance

recommended_actions: Amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers to confirm underlying AD pathology
Serial neuropsychological testing to track progression
Multidisciplinary care planning including occupational therapy for visual adaptation strategies

output.interpretation: CSF analysis reveals a characteristic Alzheimer's disease biomarker profile: decreased amyloid-beta 42, decreased amyloid-beta 42/40 ratio, and elevated phosphorylated tau and total tau. The A+T+N+ biomarker profile is consistent with Alzheimer's disease continuum. Prion markers (14-3-3 protein and RT-QuIC) are negative, effectively ruling out Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. Autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel is negative. No evidence of CNS inflammation, infection, or demyelinating disease. The combination of amyloid and tau biomarker positivity in the context of posterior cortical atrophy syndrome supports a diagnosis of PCA due to underlying Alzheimer's disease pathology.

Genetic_Testing.clinical_significance: Heterozygous APOE e4 carrier. The e4 allele is the strongest genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's disease, conferring approximately 3-fold increased risk. This genotype is consistent with but not diagnostic of Alzheimer's disease.
Negative for presenilin 1 mutations associated with early-onset familial AD.
Negative for amyloid precursor protein mutations.

Genetic_Testing.value: No pathogenic variants identified. Codon 129 MM homozygosity.
Genetic_Testing.clinical_significance: No prion protein gene mutations. Codon 129 MM is the most common genotype and is associated with increased susceptibility to sporadic CJD, but in the absence of other prion disease features, this is not clinically significant.

output.interpretation: Genetic testing reveals APOE e3/e4 heterozygosity, which increases Alzheimer's disease risk but is not diagnostic in isolation. No pathogenic variants in autosomal dominant AD genes (PSEN1, PSEN2, APP) or the prion protein gene (PRNP) were identified. The early-onset AD gene panel is negative, consistent with sporadic rather than familial AD. The patient's age of onset (62) is at the boundary of early-onset (<65) and late-onset definitions. Given the positive amyloid biomarkers, the APOE e4 carrier status provides supporting evidence for Alzheimer's disease etiology.

output.query: posterior cortical atrophy diagnosis criteria Alzheimer variant

results.title: Consensus classification of posterior cortical atrophy
results.authors: Crutch SJ, Schott JM, Rabinovici GD, et al.
results.summary: International consensus criteria for PCA. Classification level 1 (PCA syndrome) requires: (1) insidious onset, (2) gradual progression, (3) prominent early visuospatial/visuoperceptual deficits, (4) relatively preserved memory and insight in early stages. Classification level 2 (PCA-pure) additionally requires absence of other conditions that better explain the syndrome. Classification level 3 (PCA-AD) requires biomarker evidence of AD pathology. Most PCA cases (~80%) have underlying AD pathology at autopsy.

results.title: Posterior cortical atrophy: a review of neuropsychological and clinical features
results.authors: Crutch SJ, Lehmann M, Schott JM, et al.
results.summary: Comprehensive review of PCA. Typical age at onset 50-65 years. Core features include Balint syndrome components (simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, oculomotor apraxia), visual agnosia, Gerstmann syndrome features, and visual field deficits. May progress to involve memory, language, and frontal functions. FDG-PET shows parieto-occipital hypometabolism. Most cases are AD at pathology.

results.title: Distinguishing posterior cortical atrophy from typical Alzheimer's disease and other dementias
results.authors: Tang-Wai DF, Graff-Radford NR, Boeve BF, et al.
results.summary: Comparison of PCA vs typical AD: PCA patients are younger at onset, have preserved memory early, and have prominent visuospatial deficits. Brain MRI shows parieto-occipital atrophy rather than hippocampal atrophy. PCA can be misdiagnosed as psychiatric illness or ophthalmological disease.

output.summary: Posterior cortical atrophy (PCA) is a neurodegenerative syndrome characterized by progressive visuospatial and visuoperceptual dysfunction with relative preservation of episodic memory in early stages. It most commonly represents an atypical phenotypic variant of Alzheimer's disease (~80% of cases at autopsy). Consensus diagnostic criteria (Crutch et al., 2017) define three classification levels: syndrome (clinical features), PCA-pure (exclusion of other causes), and PCA-AD (biomarker confirmation). Typical onset is age 50-65, younger than classic AD. Key clinical features include components of Balint syndrome, Gerstmann syndrome, alexia, and apraxia. Neuroimaging shows posterior-predominant cortical atrophy and parieto-occipital hypometabolism on FDG-PET. Amyloid PET and CSF biomarkers can confirm underlying AD pathology.

output.proposed: Donepezil 5 mg daily for Alzheimer's disease
interactions: Escitalopram + Donepezil: Both can prolong QTc interval. Monitor ECG. Risk is generally low at standard doses but additive QTc prolongation is possible.
warnings: Donepezil may cause bradycardia; monitor heart rate, especially in combination with other medications that can slow heart rate
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, anorexia) are common; start at 5 mg daily and increase to 10 mg after 4-6 weeks if tolerated
May worsen urinary obstruction symptoms if present
Monitor for vivid dreams/nightmares; consider morning dosing if sleep disruption occurs
output.formulary_status: Formulary; covered by most insurance plans including Medicare Part D
alternatives: Rivastigmine (patch or oral) — alternative cholinesterase inhibitor, may have fewer GI side effects with transdermal formulation
Galantamine — alternative cholinesterase inhibitor with nicotinic receptor modulation
Memantine — NMDA receptor antagonist, typically added for moderate-severe AD but sometimes used earlier in combination

output.query: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease vs Alzheimer's disease differential diagnosis

results.title: Rapidly progressive dementias: prions, prion-like disorders, and non-prion etiologies
results.summary: Key differentiating features: CJD progresses from onset to akinetic mutism in weeks to months (median survival 5 months for sporadic CJD). AD progresses over years. CJD features include rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, periodic sharp wave complexes on EEG, and cortical/basal ganglia restricted diffusion on MRI DWI. AD does not show restricted diffusion. CSF 14-3-3 protein and RT-QuIC assay are highly specific for CJD.

results.title: MRI in Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease
results.authors: Vitali P, Maccagnano E, Caverzasi E, et al.
results.summary: MRI DWI showing cortical ribboning and/or caudate/putamen restricted diffusion is highly sensitive (>90%) and specific for sporadic CJD. The absence of restricted diffusion on DWI effectively makes CJD very unlikely. In AD, MRI shows atrophy patterns without restricted diffusion.

output.summary: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease and Alzheimer's disease can be differentiated by tempo of decline (weeks-months vs years), presence of myoclonus and periodic sharp wave complexes on EEG in CJD, cortical ribboning on MRI DWI in CJD (absent in AD), and CSF biomarkers (14-3-3 and RT-QuIC positive in CJD, negative in AD; amyloid/tau profile characteristic in AD). This patient's 8-month gradual progression without myoclonus, normal EEG without periodic discharges, absence of restricted diffusion on MRI, and negative 14-3-3/RT-QuIC strongly argue against CJD.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy due to Alzheimer's disease (atypical AD variant)

differential.diagnosis: Posterior cortical atrophy due to Alzheimer's disease
differential.key_features: Progressive visuospatial and visuoperceptual deficits (Balint syndrome, Gerstmann syndrome features, alexia, apraxia) with relatively preserved episodic memory. Posterior-predominant cortical atrophy on MRI. Positive amyloid biomarkers. Age and profile consistent with PCA-AD.

Can present with visuospatial deficits and visual hallucinations, but this patient lacks core LBD features: no visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism, no REM sleep behavior disorder, no cognitive fluctuations.
Can cause posterior cortical symptoms and apraxia, but typically asymmetric with limb rigidity, dystonia, alien limb phenomenon, and cortical sensory loss. This patient lacks motor findings.

Rapidly progressive dementia with myoclonus, periodic sharp wave complexes on EEG, and cortical/basal ganglia restricted diffusion on MRI DWI. This patient has gradual 8-month progression without myoclonus, normal EEG, and no restricted diffusion — effectively excluding CJD.

White matter disease is present (Fazekas 2) and could contribute to symptoms, but the posterior-predominant cortical atrophy pattern, progressive course, and visuospatial syndrome are not typical of pure vascular dementia. Vascular risk factors are minimal.

While anxiety is present and the sister has psychiatric symptoms, the objective visuospatial deficits on neuropsychological testing and structural posterior atrophy on MRI are inconsistent with a purely functional etiology.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history and neurological examination focusing on visuospatial and visuoperceptual function (Balint syndrome features, Gerstmann syndrome, visual agnosia, apraxia)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive visuospatial dysfunction with relatively preserved memory, features of Balint and Gerstmann syndromes

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric analysis to assess pattern of cortical atrophy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant parieto-occipital atrophy with relative hippocampal preservation

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup to exclude reversible causes of dementia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic, thyroid, B12, and infectious screening — no reversible cause identified

optimal_actions.action: Request comprehensive neuropsychological testing to characterize the cognitive profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severe visuospatial/visuoperceptual deficits with relatively preserved verbal memory and language fluency, consistent with posterior cortical atrophy

optimal_actions.action: Order amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers to confirm underlying Alzheimer's disease pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive amyloid PET scan or decreased CSF amyloid-beta 42 with elevated phospho-tau, confirming AD pathology

optimal_actions.action: Consider FDG-PET to demonstrate posterior-predominant hypometabolism pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral parieto-occipital hypometabolism with preserved frontal and medial temporal metabolism

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on posterior cortical atrophy diagnostic criteria and management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Consensus PCA diagnostic criteria, treatment approaches, prognosis information

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions before initiating cholinesterase inhibitor therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: QTc monitoring recommended with escitalopram + donepezil combination; GI side effects expected

optimal_actions.action: Consider genetic testing (APOE, early-onset AD gene panel) given age <65 at onset
optimal_actions.expected_finding: APOE e4 carrier status providing additional risk evidence; no autosomal dominant AD mutations

optimal_actions.action: Recommend occupational therapy for visual adaptation strategies and safety assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Environmental modifications and compensatory strategies for visuospatial deficits

critical_actions: Recognize that the presentation represents posterior cortical atrophy rather than a primary ophthalmological problem or psychiatric disorder
Order brain MRI to identify posterior-predominant atrophy pattern
Obtain neuropsychological testing demonstrating the dissociation between impaired visuospatial function and preserved memory
Confirm Alzheimer's disease pathology with amyloid biomarkers (PET or CSF)
Rule out Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease given the differential — note absence of rapidly progressive course, myoclonus, and restricted diffusion on MRI
Screen for reversible causes of dementia with standard laboratory workup
contraindicated_actions: Attributing all symptoms to anxiety or depression and treating only with psychiatric medications without further neurological workup
Diagnosing a primary ophthalmological condition and deferring neurological evaluation
Initiating antipsychotic medications without clear indication (risk of harm, particularly if LBD is still in differential)
Performing invasive brain biopsy before exhausting non-invasive diagnostic testing
Diagnosing Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease based on the subacute timeline without supporting MRI (DWI cortical ribboning), EEG (periodic discharges), or CSF (14-3-3/RT-QuIC) evidence
key_reasoning_points: The presentation of progressive visuospatial dysfunction (difficulty reading, navigating, recognizing faces, dressing) with relatively preserved episodic memory is the hallmark of posterior cortical atrophy, not typical amnestic Alzheimer's disease
Posterior cortical atrophy is most commonly caused by Alzheimer's disease pathology (~80% of cases) but presents atypically with visuospatial rather than memory-predominant symptoms

The source case involved CJD, which can be distinguished from AD-PCA by: rapid progression (weeks-months vs months-years), myoclonus, periodic sharp wave complexes on EEG, and cortical/basal ganglia restricted diffusion on MRI DWI — none of which are present in this case

The white matter disease (Fazekas 2) on MRI is a red herring that could suggest vascular cognitive impairment, but the posterior-predominant cortical atrophy pattern and progressive visuospatial syndrome are not explained by small vessel disease alone

The family history of a sister with recent irritability and anxiety could suggest a genetic neurodegenerative process or serve as a red herring for prion disease, but the clinical profile clearly favors AD-PCA
The normal EEG (no periodic discharges, no triphasic waves) and absence of restricted diffusion on MRI DWI are key negative findings that effectively exclude prion disease

Confirmation of AD pathology requires biomarker evidence: amyloid PET (elevated cortical amyloid) or CSF (decreased Aβ42, elevated phospho-tau), which establishes the diagnosis as PCA-AD (classification level 3 per consensus criteria)

metadata.condition_name: Early-stage Alzheimer's disease (posterior cortical atrophy variant)
metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle: Patient presents with progressive visuospatial deficits mimicking ophthalmological disease, not classic memory loss. The source case (CJD) introduces a prion disease differential that must be systematically excluded. White matter disease on MRI suggests vascular etiology as a red herring. Family history with a sister developing psychiatric symptoms could hint at prion or other genetic disease. The correct diagnosis of posterior cortical atrophy as an atypical Alzheimer's variant requires recognizing the characteristic cognitive dissociation and confirming with amyloid biomarkers.
